proposals.novelty: While network approaches exist, applying the ecological analogy of latent 'abiotic' drivers (neuro-behavioral traits) alongside dynamic symptom-symptom interactions to directly challenge the primacy of belief content in cognitive therapy models is a novel integration.

proposals.title: Beyond Beliefs: A Network Analysis of Symptom, Behaviour, and Attention Factors in Differentiating Social Anxiety and PTSD in Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Cognitive models, central to Clark's work, posit that disorder-specific belief content is primary in differentiating and treating conditions like social anxiety disorder (SAD) and PTSD. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests shared transdiagnostic processes (e.g., avoidance, attentional bias) and similar response to generic CBT protocols. This raises a critical question: are beliefs the core differentiating factor, or do other mechanistic variables like behavioural responses or attentional patterns play a more primary role in disorder manifestation and treatment?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesise that network structures of symptoms, safety behaviours, and attentional bias data will differentiate adolescent SAD and PTSD more robustly than networks centred on disorder-specific belief content alone, suggesting that belief differences may be an emergent property of other primary factors.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of combined data from Clark's OPTYC trial (PTSD) and similar adolescent SAD therapy trials. Using Gaussian Graphical Model (GGM) network analysis with EBICglasso model selection, we will construct separate cross-sectional networks for each disorder. Networks will include nodes measuring: 1) disorder-specific beliefs (e.g., Posttraumatic Cognitions Inventory, Social Thoughts and Beliefs Scale), 2) symptoms (disorder-specific and general anxiety), 3) behavioural markers (e.g., safety behaviours, avoidance), and 4) attentional bias scores from dot-probe tasks. We will compare network structures, centrality indices (e.g., strength, predictability), and the relative position of belief nodes using permutation-based network comparison tests.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical test of belief primacy in differentiating SAD and PTSD within a network psychometrics framework. It will clarify whether treatment should prioritise modifying belief content directly or focus more on interrupting behavioural and attentional maintenance cycles. The findings could inform more parsimonious, mechanism-focused transdiagnostic interventions for youth anxiety and trauma.
proposals.novelty: While network analysis has been applied to symptoms, this study innovatively integrates belief, behaviour, and cognitive process data within the same model to test a foundational assumption of cognitive theory. It directly pits belief-centric against a multi-mechanism model of disorder differentiation using Clark's own high-fidelity therapy trial datasets.

proposals.title: A Pre-Registered, Multi-Lab Test of the Cognitive Model: Coordinated Validation in Social Anxiety, PTSD, and Adolescent Anxiety
proposals.motivation: The cognitive model is foundational to CBT, but its core predictions about disorder-specific cognitive content and change mechanisms are often tested post-hoc or in single-disorder studies, limiting rigorous evaluation of its generalizability. A coordinated, pre-registered, and falsifiable test across multiple disorders is needed to strengthen the model's scientific credibility and clarify its boundaries, informing more precise and effective treatments.
proposals.hypothesis: The core cognitive model predicts that disorder-specific intrusive negative thoughts and their appraisals will show a specific temporal and correlational relationship with symptom severity, and that early change in these cognitive variables will predict subsequent symptom reduction across social anxiety disorder (SAD), post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), and adolescent generalized anxiety disorder (GAD).
proposals.method: We will conduct a coordinated series of therapy process studies across three independent labs. Each lab will treat one target disorder (SAD, PTSD, adolescent GAD) using a validated CBT protocol (e.g., Clark & Wells for SAD; TF-CBT for PTSD). We will pre-register specific predictions on the Open Science Framework regarding cross-lagged panel models linking weekly measures of disorder-specific cognitions (e.g., social threat appraisals, trauma-related negative beliefs, intolerance of uncertainty) and symptoms. A central analysis plan will define falsification criteria (e.g., non-significant cross-lagged paths, weaker cognitive-to-symptom than symptom-to-cognitive prediction).
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first simultaneous, pre-registered test of the cognitive model's core predictions across distinct disorders, moving beyond narrative reviews. It will generate a public benchmark for model validation and identify potential boundary conditions or necessary modifications for specific disorders, directly informing the development of more mechanistically focused interventions.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its explicit pre-registration of falsification criteria for a theoretical model in clinical psychology and its use of a coordinated multi-lab, multi-disorder design to test generalizability, moving from post-hoc model fitting to prospective, risky prediction.

proposals.title: Real-Time Triggers of Social Threat Schemas: A Multi-Method Study of Implicit and Explicit Activation in Adolescents with Social Anxiety
proposals.motivation: Cognitive models of anxiety and PTSD posit that maladaptive schemas drive symptoms, but their activation is typically assessed via retrospective self-report, which is prone to bias. A mechanistic, real-time understanding of schema triggers is crucial for refining interventions like cognitive therapy. This is especially pertinent for adolescents, a key developmental period for social anxiety, where early, precise intervention could alter long-term trajectories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that maladaptive social threat schemas in adolescents with social anxiety are activated by subtle, non-conscious environmental cues (e.g., brief ambiguous facial expressions) prior to or in the absence of conscious awareness, and that these implicit activations, measured via psychophysiology and behavioral tasks, will predict subsequent negative affect and safety behaviors more strongly than explicit self-reported triggers.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 28-day micro-longitudinal study with 60 adolescents (15-18yrs) with social anxiety disorder. Participants will complete baseline implicit association tests (IAT) for social threat and wear a wrist-based device (e.g., Empatica E4) for continuous heart rate and electrodermal activity monitoring. Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) via smartphone will prompt 5x daily for explicit schema activation events. Crucially, twice daily, a custom app will administer a brief implicit behavioral task (a rapid facial affect recognition task with morphed expressions) to probe schema reactivity to subtle social cues. Multi-level modelling will test temporal precedence and predictive power of implicit vs. explicit measures.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated, real-time model of implicit and explicit schema activation in adolescent social anxiety. It will directly inform the 'why and when' of using behavioural experiments and cognitive restructuring in therapy by identifying the most potent triggers. The protocol also offers a blueprint for objective, multi-method assessment that could be adapted for PTSD and other disorders.
proposals.novelty: While EMA and physiology are used separately, their combined use with scheduled implicit behavioural probes to capture schema reactivity to standardized subtle cues is novel. This moves beyond correlating self-report with physiology to experimentally testing for dissociations between conscious and non-conscious precursors in naturalistic settings.

proposals.title: Mapping Cognitive Danger Signals: An Immunological Analogue for Identifying Specific Triggers of Maladaptive Schema Activation in Social Anxiety
proposals.motivation: Cognitive models posit that maladaptive schemas underlie disorders like social anxiety and PTSD, but current understanding of what precisely triggers their acute activation is imprecise, relying on broad categories like 'social evaluation'. This limits the development of targeted interventions. Drawing an analogy from immunology—where specific danger signals (e.g., PAMPs/DAMPs) initiate cascades—offers a novel framework to achieve a more mechanistic, granular understanding of schema activation.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, identifiable 'cognitive danger signals' (CDSs), comprising precise configurations of external cues and internal appraisals, function as high-affinity triggers for maladaptive social threat schemas, initiating a predictable cognitive-affective-behavioral cascade analogous to an inflammatory signaling pathway.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-method, longitudinal study with adolescents (N=120) with social anxiety disorder, integrating Clark's survey methodology and experience sampling. First, idiographic surveys will define each participant's core feared social outcomes. Then, a 21-day ecological momentary assessment (EMA) protocol will capture real-time data on external events, appraisals, affect, and safety behaviors. Using multilevel pattern analysis and network psychometrics, we will identify the specific cue-appraisal constellations (CDSs) that most reliably precede acute anxiety spikes, modeling their temporal associations with subsequent symptoms.
proposals.contribution: This will produce a fine-grained taxonomy of schema triggers, moving beyond generic labels to operationalized CDSs. It will delineate the early 'signaling pathway' of schema activation, offering new targets for cognitive therapy (e.g., pre-emptive intervention on CDS detection) and informing the personalization of internet-based CBT protocols like OPTYC by identifying individual-specific activation sequences.
proposals.novelty: While schema theory is well-established, applying an immunological danger signal paradigm to define triggers with high specificity is novel. The approach shifts focus from schema content to its precise activation mechanism, a level of mechanistic detail not yet pursued in cognitive clinical science.

proposals.title: Targeting Modes and Dual Processing in Social Anxiety: A Randomised Controlled Trial of a Novel Cognitive Therapy Intervention for Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Cognitive models of social anxiety, including Clark and Wells' (1995) model, highlight the role of maladaptive 'modes' (e.g., self-as-social-object), attentional focus, and dual processing (simultaneous processing of internal sensations and external social cues). However, standard CBT techniques may not explicitly or systematically target these specific components. Developing and testing focused interventions for these mechanisms could enhance treatment efficacy, particularly for adolescents where these processes are salient.
proposals.hypothesis: A cognitive therapy protocol that explicitly targets the deactivation of the 'anxious social self' mode, redirects attentional focus externally, and trains integrated (non-dual) processing will lead to greater reductions in social anxiety symptoms and post-event processing in adolescents compared to standard CBT.
proposals.method: We will conduct a parallel-group, randomised controlled trial (RCT) comparing the novel intervention to an active control (standard CBT for social anxiety). Participants (N=120 adolescents, aged 14-18, with a primary diagnosis of Social Anxiety Disorder) will be randomly allocated. The novel intervention will integrate: 1) Mode work using experiential techniques (e.g., imagery rescripting) to access and update the self-concept, 2) Attentional training using bespoke video-based tasks to practice external focus, and 3) Dual-processing disruption via in-session behavioural experiments designed to integrate sensory processing. Primary outcomes will be clinician-rated social anxiety severity and self-reported post-event processing at post-treatment and 6-month follow-up.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first empirical test of a therapy package specifically designed to modify the core components of modes, attention, and dual processing in social anxiety. It will advance theoretical understanding of key mechanisms of change and, if effective, offer a more targeted and potent treatment option for a disorder known for its persistence.
proposals.novelty: While Clark and Wells' model is well-established, directly translating its components of 'modes' and 'dual processing' into a structured, manualised therapy protocol for adolescents is novel. The integration of imagery-based mode work with real-time attentional and sensory integration training moves beyond standard cognitive restructuring and exposure.

proposals.title: Computational Modelling of the Attentional Blink as a Transdiagnostic Marker of Maladaptive Cognitive Rigidity in Anxiety and PTSD
proposals.motivation: Cognitive models of anxiety and PTSD posit that maladaptive, rigid attention to threat contributes to symptom maintenance. The attentional blink (AB) paradigm, a robust marker of temporal attention, offers a quantifiable model of how adaptive focus can degrade into maladaptive rigidity, but its parameters have not been formally linked to clinical symptomatology. This study aims to bridge this gap by using computational modelling within a clinical framework to identify specific attentional dysregulations predictive of disorder.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the AB reflects a continuum of attentional control, where specific computational parameters (e.g., excessive inhibition strength, prolonged recovery time) will be dysregulated in clinical anxiety and PTSD, and that the degree of this dysregulation will correlate with symptom severity, particularly for emotionally salient stimuli.
proposals.method: We will conduct a two-stream study. First, a behavioural experiment with clinical and non-clinical participants will employ a standard RSVP AB task, with neutral and threat-related distractor words. Second, we will fit participant data to a pre-2026 computational model of the AB, such as the Boost and Bounce or Two-Stage Competition model, to extract parameters like attentional enhancement and suppression. We will use linear mixed-effects models to test associations between these parameters, clinical diagnosis (anxiety/PTSD), and symptom scores from standardised measures (e.g., Social Phobia Inventory, PTSD Checklist).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, computationally precise marker of maladaptive attentional rigidity, moving beyond descriptive behavioural effects. It will directly test a core mechanism from cognitive models of anxiety disorders. The findings could inform the development of targeted attention-modification components for cognitive therapy, particularly in digital formats, by identifying which specific attentional parameters to train.
proposals.novelty: While the AB is well-studied, applying formal computational modelling to its parameters within a clinical sample to predict symptomatology is novel. This fusion of cognitive neuroscience methods with clinical psychological theory offers a more mechanistic test of cognitive models than correlation of raw behavioural scores alone.

proposals.title: Real-Time Validation of a Transdiagnostic Cognitive Model: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study of Schema Activation and Mode Shifts in Anxiety, PTSD, and Depression
proposals.motivation: Cognitive models, such as Clark and Wells's (1995) model of social anxiety, are powerful but often validated retrospectively or in the lab, limiting ecological validity. The transdiagnostic applicability of core mechanisms like schema activation and 'mode' shifts across disorders like GAD, PTSD, and depression remains inadequately tested in daily life. Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) offers a method to capture these dynamic processes as they unfold, addressing a critical gap in model validation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, disorder-relevant triggers captured via EMA will predict real-time schema activation and subsequent shifts to dysfunctional 'modes' (e.g., threat, vulnerable child), and that these dynamic sequences will show both transdiagnostic commonalities and disorder-specific patterns.
proposals.method: We will recruit clinical samples (adolescents and young adults) with primary diagnoses of social anxiety disorder, PTSD, or major depression. Participants will complete a baseline assessment of core schemas and then undertake a 14-day EMA protocol via smartphone. Surveys will be triggered 5 times daily (signal-contingent) and after self-reported stressful events (event-contingent). Items will assess current affect, triggers, schema-related cognitions (e.g., "I am in danger," "I am unlovable"), and behavioural/physiological responses. Data will be analyzed using multilevel vector autoregressive (mlVAR) modelling to map temporal networks of symptom and schema interactions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first ecologically valid, real-time test of a dynamic cognitive model across multiple disorders. It will identify precise, momentary pathways maintaining psychopathology, informing more targeted, mechanistic interventions. The resulting data will directly refine the model itself, highlighting core transdiagnostic versus disorder-specific treatment targets.
proposals.novelty: While EMA is used in psychopathology research, its application to validate and iteratively refine a specific cognitive model in a transdiagnostic clinical sample is novel. The fusion of Clark's cognitive clinical science with intensive longitudinal network analysis moves beyond static, disorder-specific model testing.

proposals.title: A Computational Phenotyping Study of Adaptive and Maladaptive Anxiety in Adolescents: Predicting Functional Impairment and Clinical Progression
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research indicates that subclinical anxiety in adolescents is a major risk factor for later psychiatric disorders, but it remains unclear why some high-anxiety individuals function well while others progress to clinical impairment. Current diagnostic tools cannot reliably decompose anxiety into its functional (adaptive, flexible) and dysfunctional (maladaptive, rigid) components, hindering early, targeted preventive interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a computational model integrating belief-updating parameters from a reversal learning task with psychophysiological (skin conductance, heart rate variability) and ecological momentary assessment (EMA) data will successfully discriminate adaptive from maladaptive anxiety profiles. Specifically, the maladaptive profile, characterized by rigid prior beliefs and poor physiological regulation, will predict greater real-world functional impairment and a higher rate of progression to a clinical anxiety or PTSD diagnosis over 12 months.
proposals.method: We will recruit 200 adolescents (aged 14-18) with stable high trait anxiety and intermittent sub-threshold symptoms via longitudinal school screening. Participants will complete a probabilistic reversal learning task; their performance will be modeled using a Hierarchical Gaussian Filter (HGF) to estimate individual belief-updating parameters (e.g., learning rates, surprise sensitivity). This will be combined with task-based psychophysiology and a 14-day EMA protocol assessing anxiety, context, and avoidance. A machine learning classifier (e.g., Support Vector Machine) will be trained on this multi-modal data to classify anxiety profiles, with outcomes being functional impairment and diagnostic status at 12-month follow-up.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, multi-modal computational phenotype for maladaptive anxiety, moving beyond symptom counts to mechanistic processes. It will directly test the predictive validity of this model for real-world impairment and clinical progression, offering a tool for identifying high-risk adolescents who would benefit most from preventive cognitive therapy, such as the online CBT protocols developed in the OPTYC trial.
proposals.novelty: While computational psychiatry has modeled anxiety, no pre-2026 study has prospectively used a multi-modal HGF model to decompose anxiety in a subclinical adolescent cohort and link it to longitudinal clinical outcomes, bridging laboratory tasks, real-world monitoring, and preventive mental health frameworks.

proposals.title: Sentinel Surveillance of Anxiety: Testing the Scalability of a Cognitive Model to High-Risk Subclinical Populations
proposals.motivation: Cognitive models of anxiety disorders, such as Clark and Wells's (1995) model for social anxiety, are well-validated in clinical populations but their applicability to subclinical anxiety is less clear. Understanding the early, sub-threshold maintenance of anxiety is crucial for improving prevention and early intervention strategies. This study adapts an efficient epidemiological method to test model scalability, addressing a key gap in translating clinical science to public mental health.
proposals.hypothesis: The core cognitive and behavioral maintenance processes specified in established clinical models of anxiety (e.g., negative self-focused attention, safety behaviors, biased post-event processing) will be present, inter-correlated, and predictive of anxiety severity in strategically selected subclinical, high-anxiety 'sentinel' groups, supporting the model's generalizability beyond clinical thresholds.
proposals.method: We will employ a cross-sectional, multi-site sentinel surveillance design, recruiting 300-400 participants from high-stress, high-anxiety subclinical populations (e.g., medical interns, law students, junior barristers). Participants will complete validated self-report measures of anxiety symptoms (e.g., DASS-21, LSAS-SR) and detailed online surveys, adapted from Clark's prior work, quantifying key model constructs: frequency of safety behaviors, degree of self-focused attention, and intensity of negative post-event processing. Data will be analyzed using structural equation modeling (SEM) to test the fit of the hypothesized maintenance model to the subclinical data, comparing it to fit indices from known clinical samples.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a methodologically efficient test of whether clinical cognitive models scale down to explain subclinical anxiety, potentially validating a 'sentinel' approach for modeling psychological processes. It will generate novel insights into the early development and maintenance of anxiety, informing the design of targeted preventive cognitive-behavioral interventions for at-risk groups.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the conceptual adaptation of the public health 'sentinel surveillance' methodology to cognitive clinical psychology, offering a strategic and powerful alternative to large-scale population studies for testing model scalability. This approach directly bridges epidemiological efficiency with detailed cognitive model testing in a way not previously attempted in anxiety research.

proposals.title: Passive Digital Phenotyping of Latent Anxiety States and Safety Behaviors in Social Anxiety Disorder: A Machine Learning Approach
proposals.motivation: Cognitive models of anxiety, such as Clark and Wells' (1995) model of social anxiety, posit that subtle, momentary shifts in self-focused attention, negative self-beliefs, and safety behaviors maintain disorder. However, testing these models has been limited by the reactivity and sparsity of traditional self-report methods. Intensive longitudinal assessment (e.g., ecological momentary assessment) improves ecological validity but remains burdensome and intrusive, potentially altering the very processes under study.
proposals.hypothesis: That a multimodal stream of passive sensor data (e.g., heart rate variability, vocal prosody, movement, phone usage) can be used to train machine learning models that accurately infer latent anxiety-related cognitive states (approximating 'micro-beliefs') and detect the occurrence of subtle safety behaviors, using sparse, strategically timed self-reports as ground truth labels.
proposals.method: We will recruit N=80 adolescents with social anxiety disorder. Participants will wear a consumer-grade fitness tracker (e.g., Empatica E4) and carry a smartphone running a custom sensing app for two weeks. The app will collect accelerometer, GPS, screen events, and audio snippets (processed locally for prosodic features). Sparse, context-triggered self-reports (2-5/day) will capture momentary anxiety, self-focused attention, and safety behavior use. We will use a hybrid machine learning architecture: a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network to model temporal dependencies in the sensor fusion data, trained to predict the self-reported latent states. A separate Random Forest classifier will be trained on feature-engineered sensor segments to detect discrete safety behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, ecologically valid method for testing core predictions of cognitive models of anxiety at a moment-to-moment level. It will generate the first high-density, objective timelines of latent cognitive states and safety behaviors, offering new insights into their dynamic interplay in real-world settings. The resulting model could eventually inform just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) in cognitive therapy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used sensors to detect generalized anxiety or stress, this proposal uniquely targets the inference of specific, theory-driven latent cognitive constructs (micro-beliefs) and subtle behavioral markers (safety behaviors) central to Clark and Wells' model, moving beyond coarse arousal detection.

proposals.title: Modeling Real-Time Anxiety Emergence: A Dynamical Network Analysis of Micro-Beliefs and Schemas in Social Anxiety
proposals.motivation: Cognitive models of anxiety posit that maladaptive schemas drive symptoms, but they struggle to explain the moment-by-moment fluctuations in state anxiety observed in daily life. Pre-2026, ecological momentary assessment (EMA) has captured these dynamics, but analytical methods (e.g., multilevel models) treat beliefs as static traits rather than interacting, time-varying components. A computational framework is needed to model how transient cognitive appraisals coalesce into clinical symptoms in real time.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that acute anxiety states emerge from the coordinated activation of context-specific 'micro-beliefs' (e.g., 'I will stumble over my words'), which form a time-varying network whose connectivity and energy propagate to energize a latent, stable maladaptive schema (e.g., 'I am socially inadequate'), triggering a cognitive 'phase transition' into high anxiety.
proposals.method: Adolescents with social anxiety disorder (N=60) will complete a 14-day EMA protocol, prompted 8 times daily to report state anxiety, safety behaviors, and endorsement of 10-12 pre-identified idiographic micro-beliefs. We will apply time-varying vector autoregression (tv-VAR) and graphical lasso models to estimate the moment-to-moment directed connectivity and co-activation strength between micro-belief nodes. This dynamic network will be coupled to a latent schema variable using a latent change score model, testing if periods of high network density and positive feedback predict schema activation and anxiety spikes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first computational model of real-time cognitive symptom formation in anxiety, moving from descriptive correlation to a testable, mechanistic account. It will identify potential 'hub' micro-beliefs and critical network states that could be novel targets for just-in-time adaptive interventions (JITAIs) in cognitive therapy.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying dynamical systems and network psychometrics to the micro-processes of cognition within individuals over time, moving beyond static cross-sectional network models or schema lists to model the physics-like 'energizing' of psychopathology.

proposals.title: Micro-Beliefs in Motion: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study of Momentary Threat Appraisals and Safety Behaviors in Social Anxiety Disorder
proposals.motivation: Cognitive models of anxiety posit that maladaptive beliefs and safety behaviors maintain disorders, but these are typically assessed as global, stable constructs. This fails to capture the dynamic, context-dependent nature of anxiety as it unfolds in daily life. Understanding these micro-level processes is crucial for refining cognitive therapy techniques, such as behavioral experiments and thought challenging, to target real-time symptom fluctuations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within-person, moment-by-moment increases in specific, situationally-cued 'micro-beliefs' (e.g., 'I will sound stupid right now') will predict subsequent increases in state anxiety and the use of subtle safety behaviors (e.g., rehearsing sentences, avoiding eye contact) in individuals with Social Anxiety Disorder (SAD).
proposals.method: We will conduct an Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) study with 60 adolescents and young adults diagnosed with SAD. Using a smartphone app, participants will receive 8 random prompts daily for 14 days. At each prompt, they will rate state anxiety and the strength of pre-identified, personalized micro-beliefs (drawn from an initial cognitive assessment). They will also report safety behavior use since the last prompt. Data will be analyzed using multilevel vector autoregressive (mlVAR) modeling to examine temporal, within-person relationships between micro-beliefs, anxiety, and safety behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, temporal map of how specific cognitive and behavioral processes interact to maintain social anxiety in real-world settings. It will directly inform the development of 'just-in-time' cognitive therapy interventions delivered via mobile health platforms, making treatment more responsive to momentary needs. The findings will also offer a novel methodology for testing the micro-validity of cognitive-behavioral models.
proposals.novelty: While EMA has been used to study anxiety, this proposal uniquely focuses on testing the causal, within-person links between highly specific, personalized 'micro-beliefs' (rather than general negative affect or global beliefs) and real-time safety behaviors, as predicted by Clark and Wells' (1995) model.

proposals.title: Inferring Belief Maintenance Systems from Safety Behaviors: A Computational Model for Personalizing CBT for Anxiety Disorders
proposals.motivation: Cognitive models of anxiety posit that maladaptive core beliefs are maintained by safety behaviors, but current CBT assessment relies on self-report, which is limited. A formal computational method to infer the latent belief structure and the specific maintenance 'rules' from observable behavior is lacking. This gap hinders the development of precise, mechanistically targeted interventions that could disrupt maladaptive maintenance cycles, particularly in social anxiety and PTSD.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an individual's pattern of safety behaviors and anxiety symptoms can be accurately modeled as the output of a specific, inferable belief maintenance system. Further, we predict that maladaptive systems will be characterized by rigid, threat-confirming update rules, whereas adaptive systems will employ flexible, evidence-sensitive principles.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian inverse model. The 'generative model' will simulate how a set of core beliefs (latent variables) and a maintenance policy (a set of update rules) produce safety behaviors and anxiety ratings. Using data from existing clinical trials (e.g., OPTYC), we will apply variational inference algorithms (e.g., Stochastic Variational Inference) to solve the inverse problem: estimating the most likely beliefs and maintenance rules for each participant from their pre-treatment behavioral data. Model comparison (e.g., WAIC) will identify distinct classes of maintenance systems.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first computational framework to jointly quantify core beliefs and their maintenance mechanisms from behavior, moving beyond descriptive models. It will yield a novel diagnostic tool to classify patients by their maintenance system type. The resulting 'blueprint' of a maladaptive system will directly inform the design of personalized CBT modules aimed at disrupting specific maintenance rules.
proposals.novelty: While computational psychiatry models beliefs, it rarely models the active *principles* that maintain them. This work uniquely frames the therapeutic challenge as an engineering problem of system identification and reconfiguration, directly linking inferred cognitive mechanics to intervention design in a way not previously attempted.

proposals.title: Adversarial Stress-Testing of Cognitive Architectures: A Framework for Evaluating Adaptive Belief Maintenance in Simulated High-Uncertainty Environments
proposals.motivation: Cognitive models in clinical psychology, such as those for anxiety and PTSD, posit that maladaptive belief maintenance underlies psychopathology, but these principles are rarely tested in a formal, falsifiable manner. An independent, operationalized benchmark for adaptive belief maintenance, derived from pre-2026 clinical science, is needed to rigorously evaluate and compare different cognitive architectures. This would allow the field to move beyond descriptive theory towards identifying which computational principles best predict resilience or vulnerability under stress.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that when subjected to a standardized adversarial testing framework, cognitive architectures incorporating principles from cognitive therapy (e.g., belief updating based on disconfirmatory evidence, meta-cognitive awareness) will demonstrate superior performance on metrics of adaptive function (e.g., calibration, flexibility) compared to alternative architectures, particularly in simulated high-uncertainty and belief-perturbation scenarios.
proposals.method: We will first formalize a benchmark framework for adaptive belief maintenance, operationalizing clinical constructs (e.g., cognitive flexibility, decentering, probability overestimation) into quantifiable metrics. We will then implement this framework in a simulation environment to conduct adversarial tests on three architectures: a Clark & Wells-inspired social anxiety model, a Rescorla-Wagner-based model, and a Bayesian inference model. Tests will include longitudinal exposure to ambiguous social feedback (simulating social anxiety contexts) and sudden threat cues (simulating PTSD triggers), measuring outcomes like calibration drift and behavioral avoidance proxies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the field with a novel, external benchmark for evaluating the adaptive capacity of cognitive models, moving from correlation to causal stress-testing. It will generate falsifiable evidence for which computational principles best confer resilience, directly informing the development of more robust, mechanistically precise digital interventions like internet-based cognitive therapy (ICT) for anxiety disorders.
proposals.novelty: While cognitive models are common, subjecting them to a unified, adversarial testing regimen derived from clinical benchmarks is novel. This approach bridges computational psychiatry and clinical trial methodology, applying a 'stress-test' paradigm—inspired by protocol-based studies like OPTYC—to basic model architectures.

proposals.title: Modelling Cognitive Homeostasis: A Computational Immunological Framework for Belief Regulation in Anxiety Disorders
proposals.motivation: Cognitive models of anxiety disorders, such as Clark and Wells's model of social anxiety, posit that maladaptive beliefs are central to symptom maintenance. However, these models lack a formal account of the dynamic regulatory processes that normally prevent the acquisition and escalation of such beliefs, or that fail in psychopathology. Drawing an analogy from immunology—a mature field with precise models of self-regulation—offers a novel framework to specify these homeostatic cognitive mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that healthy belief regulation can be formally modeled as a dual-layer tolerance system, analogous to central and peripheral immune tolerance, and that parameters of this model will differentiate adaptive from maladaptive (e.g., anxiety-provoking) belief dynamics observed in clinical and non-clinical populations.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational cognitive model using a Bayesian belief updating architecture. 'Central tolerance' will be implemented as strong, developmentally set priors against beliefs with extreme emotional valence. 'Peripheral tolerance' will be modeled as context-sensitive meta-cognitive rules (e.g., prediction error gating, explanatory coherence competition) that suppress or compartmentalize dissonant beliefs. The model will be fitted to time-series belief data from existing therapy trials (e.g., OPTYC, sudden gains studies) to identify parameter profiles linked to treatment response and symptom flare-ups.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, testable model of cognitive homeostasis, moving beyond descriptive accounts. It will generate specific, measurable predictions about belief regulation failures in anxiety and PTSD, potentially identifying new targets for cognitive therapy, such as strengthening 'peripheral' regulatory processes.
proposals.novelty: While cognitive science uses neural and Bayesian analogies, applying the specific principles of immunological tolerance—central deletion, anergy, and regulatory suppression—to the belief system is a novel cross-disciplinary leap. It directly addresses the dynamic regulation of beliefs, not just their static content.

proposals.title: Decomposing Therapeutic Change: A Micro-Process Analysis of Cognitive Restructuring and Dual-Task Stimulation in Trauma-Focused CBT and EMDR for Adolescents with PTSD
proposals.motivation: Trauma-focused CBT (CBT-T) and EMDR are first-line treatments for PTSD, yet their active mechanisms remain inadequately understood. This limits our ability to optimize and personalize therapy, particularly for adolescents where developmental factors may influence change processes. Clarifying whether shared or distinct therapeutic processes (e.g., cognitive change, memory reconsolidation) drive symptom reduction is a critical pre-2026 research priority.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that successful outcomes in both CBT-T and EMDR will be mediated by a reduction in trauma-related cognitive distortions, but that this change will be achieved via distinct in-session micro-processes: deliberate verbal cognitive restructuring in CBT-T versus visuospatial working memory taxation during dual-task stimulation in EMDR.
proposals.method: We will conduct a detailed micro-analytic study embedded within a randomized controlled trial (RCT) comparing CBT-T and EMDR for adolescent PTSD (informed by the OPTYC trial methodology). Sessions will be video-recorded and coded using established systems (e.g., the Cognitive Change Interview, observer ratings of emotional engagement). We will employ multilevel longitudinal mediation models to test whether within-session processes (e.g., shifts in subjective units of distress [SUD] during exposure, client articulation of new perspectives) statistically mediate between-session changes in PTSD symptoms and cognitive appraisals, measured via standardized questionnaires and ecological momentary assessment (EMA).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, process-oriented comparison of CBT-T and EMDR mechanisms in youth, moving beyond efficacy to explain *how* change occurs. Findings will inform more targeted therapist training and the potential development of hybrid or modular intervention strategies that integrate the most potent active components from each approach.
proposals.novelty: While prior research has compared outcomes, this study uniquely applies a micro-process, longitudinal mediation design to directly observe and compare the putative change mechanisms of two leading trauma therapies *as they unfold in real time* within a youth sample.

proposals.title: Predicting Sudden Gains in Exposure Therapy for Social Anxiety Disorder: A Computational Modeling Approach Using Reinforcement Learning and Memory Reconsolidation
proposals.motivation: Exposure therapy is a core component of effective treatments for anxiety disorders, but individual response is highly variable and difficult to predict. Pre-2026, computational psychiatry has begun to formalize symptom mechanisms using models of associative learning, yet these have rarely been integrated with clinical trial data to forecast real-world treatment trajectories. This gap limits the personalization of cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT).
proposals.hypothesis: Individual differences in parameters of a hierarchical reinforcement learning model—specifically learning rate and inverse temperature—derived from pre-treatment behavioral tasks will predict the likelihood and magnitude of sudden gains during exposure-based therapy for social anxiety disorder.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing data from a clinical trial of cognitive therapy for social anxiety disorder (Clark et al.). Participants will complete a probabilistic reversal learning task pre-treatment. We will fit choices using a hierarchical Bayesian implementation of a Rescorla-Wagner/Pearce-Hall hybrid model to estimate individual learning rates and exploration/exploitation (inverse temperature). These parameters will be used as predictors in multilevel models of weekly symptom severity, testing their interaction with early-session exposures to forecast sudden gains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for using mechanistic cognitive models to identify which patients are most likely to benefit rapidly from exposure. It will contribute a novel, theory-driven predictor of treatment response that moves beyond static clinical questionnaires, potentially informing adaptive intervention strategies.
proposals.novelty: While computational models have been used to explain psychopathology, their direct application to predict the nuanced, nonlinear symptom changes (like sudden gains) observed in high-fidelity therapy trials is novel. This bridges a gap between laboratory-based computational cognitive science and real-world clinical process research.

ideas.title: Dual: Treating subclinical social anxiety
ideas.description: Apply the CT protocol to individuals with subclinical social anxiety to prevent disorder onset.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus fluoxetine in generalized social phobia: a randomized placebo-controlled trial.

ideas.title: Scale down to micro-beliefs and moments
ideas.description: Apply the model to moment-by-moment fluctuations in state anxiety and safety behaviors.

ideas.title: Use ecological momentary assessment for symptoms
ideas.description: Apply real-time smartphone-based assessment to capture in-vivo cognitive and anxiety states during treatment.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus exposure and applied relaxation in social phobia: A randomized controlled trial.

ideas.title: Applying Epidemiological 'Sentinel Surveillance' to Model Subclinical Anxiety
ideas.description: This idea adapts the epidemiological concept of 'sentinel surveillance' to scale a psychological model from clinical to subclinical populations. In public health, sentinel surveillance efficiently tracks disease spread by monitoring high-risk groups, providing an early model of dynamics. Similarly, we propose testing if the model explains anxiety and belief maintenance by strategically applying it to non-clinical, high-anxiety 'sentinel' groups (e.g., individuals in high-stress professions or academic settings). This provides a methodologically efficient and powerful test of the model's scalability, offering insights into early-stage or sub-threshold psychological processes before broader generalization.

ideas.title: Invert the focus from disorder to wellness
ideas.description: Study interventions that build social confidence and resilience in subclinical populations to prevent disorder.
ideas.sourceTitle: Psychological and pharmacological interventions for social anxiety disorder in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis

ideas.title: Cognitive measures are the primary mediators
ideas.description: Test if physiological or behavioral measures, not cognitive ones, are better predictors of long-term outcome.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Comparison of Cognitive Therapy, Applied Relaxation and Imipramine in the Treatment of Panic Disorder

ideas.title: Pre-registered, Falsifiable Multi-Disorder Validation of the Cognitive Model
ideas.description: This research program begins by formally specifying the core and extensible components of the cognitive model in a public pre-registration. For each target disorder (e.g., GAD, OCD, PTSD), a set of specific, measurable predictions about cognitive mechanisms and their clinical correlates is then preregistered. A coordinated series of studies across independent labs tests these predictions simultaneously, using pre-defined statistical thresholds to determine support or refutation for the model's applicability to each disorder, ensuring a rigorous and falsifiable evaluation of its generalizability.

ideas.title: Apply network analysis to symptom-maintenance factors
ideas.description: Model relationships between negative appraisals, dissociation, and PTSD symptoms as a dynamic causal network.
ideas.sourceTitle: Posttraumatic stress disorder following assault: the role of cognitive processing, trauma memory, and appraisals.

ideas.title: Scale up: Group-based cognitive therapy delivery
ideas.description: Evaluate the efficacy of this cognitive therapy program when delivered in a group format to increase access.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus exposure and applied relaxation in social phobia: A randomized controlled trial.

ideas.title: Cognitive therapy superiority is universal
ideas.description: Test if patient subgroups exist for whom exposure and applied relaxation is equally or more effective.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus exposure and applied relaxation in social phobia: A randomized controlled trial.

ideas.title: Appraisals are primary cause, not consequence
ideas.description: Test if anxiety causes problematic appraisals in HFASD, not the other way around.
ideas.sourceTitle: Social phobia and interpretation of social events.

ideas.title: Inverse Modeling of Adaptive Belief Maintenance Systems
ideas.description: This research direction develops a unified computational framework to solve the inverse problem of inferring an individual's core belief structure from their observed safety behaviors and anxiety patterns. Crucially, it extends this inference to also model the underlying 'maintenance engine'—the cognitive and behavioral principles that actively keep those beliefs stable, whether adaptively or maladaptively. This allows for the systematic comparison of maintenance systems, distinguishing those that sustain low anxiety and flexible beliefs from those that perpetuate dysfunction. The ultimate goal is to create diagnostic tools and intervention blueprints that can nudge a maladaptive belief maintenance system towards a more adaptive equilibrium.

ideas.title: Discriminating adaptive from maladaptive anxiety in high-risk populations using a predictive computational model
ideas.description: This study first identifies a high-anxiety, subclinical cohort through longitudinal screening for stable high trait anxiety plus intermittent sub-threshold symptoms. It then uses a combination of computational modeling of belief-updating tasks, psychophysiology, and real-world EMA monitoring to decompose anxiety into adaptive (flexible, context-sensitive) and maladaptive (rigid, perseverative) components. The core model is tested specifically on its ability to predict the maladaptive component and its association with real-world functional impairment and risk of progression to clinical diagnosis over a 12-month follow-up period.

ideas.title: Evaluate combined psychological and pharmacological therapy
ideas.description: Systematically assess the efficacy of combined CBT and medication compared to each monotherapy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Psychological and pharmacological interventions for social anxiety disorder in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis

ideas.title: Testing the Active Ingredient: Isolating the Self-Exposure Component in Cognitive Therapy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the efficacy of a multi-component cognitive therapy protocol is inherent to its cognitive restructuring elements, proposing instead that the behavioral 'self-exposure' homework may be the critical, necessary ingredient. Drawing an analogy from pharmacology's method for isolating active compounds, the research design adapts the 'component isolation' trial. The study would compare three conditions: full cognitive therapy with exposure homework, cognitive therapy with a placebo homework task, and a standalone self-exposure homework condition. This design allows researchers to disentangle whether therapeutic gains are driven by the cognitive therapy sessions themselves, the combination of therapy and exposure, or the exposure component alone, thereby rigorously testing the necessity of self-exposure for treatment efficacy.

ideas.title: Scale to other neurodevelopmental conditions
ideas.description: Investigate similar appraisal-emotion links in children with ADHD or social communication disorder.
ideas.sourceTitle: Social phobia and interpretation of social events.

ideas.title: Investigate self-help and digital intervention efficacy
ideas.description: Further research on guided self-help and internet-based CBT to establish their comparative effectiveness.
ideas.sourceTitle: Psychological and pharmacological interventions for social anxiety disorder in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis

ideas.title: Cost-effectiveness and implementation analysis
ideas.description: Compare the cost-effectiveness and real-world implementation feasibility of cognitive therapy versus exposure-based treatments.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus exposure and applied relaxation in social phobia: A randomized controlled trial.

ideas.title: Develop interventions targeting new model components
ideas.description: Create and evaluate therapeutic techniques focused on modes, attentional focus, and dual processing.
ideas.sourceTitle: Social Phobia: The Role of In-Situation Safety Behaviors in Maintaining Anxiety and Negative Beliefs

ideas.title: Passive Sensor Fusion for Inferring Latent Anxiety-Related States and Subtle Behaviors
ideas.description: This research system addresses the reactivity flaw of intensive self-report by using a suite of wearable and smartphone-based passive sensors to continuously capture physiological, behavioral, and environmental correlates of anxiety. Machine learning models are trained on sparse, strategically timed self-reports to infer latent states approximating 'micro-beliefs' and to detect subtle safety behaviors from sensor streams. This approach generates a high-density, ecologically valid timeline of fluctuating states without requiring constant conscious engagement from the participant. The resulting model allows for the study of moment-to-moment dynamics as they naturally occur, enabling robust testing of theoretical predictions about anxiety maintenance.

ideas.title: Differentiating Psychiatric Disorders via Network Dynamics and Latent Factors
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the primacy of beliefs in differentiating and treating disorders by drawing an analogy from systems ecology. In ecology, the field moved beyond classifying ecosystems by their most visible species to understanding the primary drivers as network interactions and latent abiotic factors. Adapting this solution, the research will investigate if factors other than beliefs—specifically, the dynamic network interactions between cognitive, affective, and physiological processes, along with latent neuro-behavioral traits—are primary in differentiating disorders and predicting treatment outcomes. This shifts the focus from belief content to system-level dynamics and hidden variables for a more foundational classification and intervention framework.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Attentional Blink as a Marker for the Continuum from Adaptive Focus to Maladaptive Rigidity
ideas.description: This research direction applies attentional blink paradigms to computational models and human participants to map the quantitative boundary between adaptive and maladaptive attentional control. By parametrically manipulating task demands and emotional salience, we can trace how a normally adaptive mechanism—suppressing distractors to focus on a target—degrades into a maladaptive failure to perceive subsequent critical stimuli, a rigidity seen in anxiety, PTSD, and depression. This allows us to model the attentional blink not just as a limitation, but as a quantifiable continuum of function, identifying specific computational parameters (e.g., inhibition strength, recovery time) whose dysregulation predicts clinical symptomatology across disorders.

ideas.title: Challenge belief primacy in disorder differentiation
ideas.description: Investigate if factors other than beliefs are primary in differentiating and treating disorders.
ideas.sourceTitle: Social Phobia: The Role of In-Situation Safety Behaviors in Maintaining Anxiety and Negative Beliefs

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Predicting treatment non-response
ideas.description: Identify baseline characteristics that predict failure in exposure therapy but success in cognitive therapy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus exposure and applied relaxation in social phobia: A randomized controlled trial.

ideas.title: Adapting a Broad-Spectrum Drug Testing Protocol to Validate a Cognitive Model Across Disorders
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the methodology from broad-spectrum drug testing in pharmacology. Just as a new antibiotic is validated against a panel of diverse bacterial pathogens, the new theoretical and applied cognitive model should be systematically tested on a pre-defined panel of psychological disorders that vary in their core cognitive mechanisms (e.g., OCD with intrusive thoughts and compulsions, GAD with pervasive worry, PTSD with trauma memory re-experiencing, MDD with negative self-schema). The analogous solution involves using standardized experimental or clinical protocols to assess the model's core predictions—such as specific attention biases, memory processes, or belief-updating functions—in each disorder. This will map the model's spectrum of applicability, identify boundaries where it fails or requires adaptation, and ultimately refine the model's general principles, moving it from a disorder-specific tool to a transdiagnostic theoretical framework.

ideas.title: Investigate mechanisms of effective trauma-focused therapies
ideas.description: Study the active components and change processes in CBT-T and EMDR to understand why they work.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy for post-traumatic stress disorder: development and evaluation.

ideas.title: Superiority of CT is not due to therapist contact
ideas.description: Control for therapist contact time to isolate specific cognitive techniques from non-specific support.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus fluoxetine in generalized social phobia: a randomized placebo-controlled trial.

ideas.title: Examine moderators of treatment response
