ideas.sourceTitle: Psychological and pharmacological interventions for social anxiety disorder in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis

ideas.title: Investigate Schema Activation via Threat Detection and Homeostatic Imbalance Analogues
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from immunology, where the triggers for maladaptive immune responses are precisely mapped, to the cognitive model of schemas. The analogous problem is understanding how latent pathological states become acutely activated. The immunological solution involves identifying specific danger signals (DAMPs/PAMPs) and their receptor bindings that initiate a cascading response. Adapted to schema theory, the research would investigate the specific external and internal triggers ('cognitive danger signals') that bind to and energize maladaptive schemas. The core work would be to define these triggers with high specificity and then delineate the subsequent cognitive-affective-behavioral 'signaling pathway' that constitutes schema activation, moving beyond broad categories to precise mechanistic understanding.

ideas.title: Test cognitive restructuring targeting specific appraisals
ideas.description: Develop and evaluate interventions that directly modify problematic appraisals like low future expectancy in HFASD.
ideas.sourceTitle: Social phobia and interpretation of social events.

ideas.title: Computational Micro-Dynamics of Belief-Schema Networks in Real-Time Anxiety
ideas.description: This research direction combines high-resolution, moment-by-moment ecological assessment of state anxiety and safety behaviors with computational network or dynamical systems models. The core innovation is modeling micro-beliefs (rapid, context-specific cognitive appraisals) as interacting nodes in a time-varying network, where their co-activation patterns and strengths determine the 'energizing' of broader, stable maladaptive schemas. This allows for the simulation of real-time symptom emergence from basic cognitive building blocks, moving beyond static schema maps to a physics-inspired model of cognitive phase transitions in psychopathology.

ideas.title: Testing if self-exposure's therapeutic efficacy is mediated by its capacity to reorganize dysfunctional cognitive modes
ideas.description: This study investigates whether the active ingredient of cognitive therapy—self-exposure homework—works by fundamentally reorganizing the patient's underlying network of cognitive modes, rather than merely modifying specific expectancies or self-evaluations. It directly tests mode theory by experimentally manipulating the presence/absence of self-exposure and measuring consequent changes in the connectivity, hierarchy, and activation patterns of cognitive modes using network analysis techniques. This approach challenges the theory's proposed organization by examining if successful treatment induces a mode reorganization not centered on the theorized core constructs, thereby linking a specific therapeutic process to a systemic cognitive change mechanism. The results could redefine the 'dose' of therapy from a behavioral task to a measurable shift in cognitive architecture.

ideas.title: Pharmacokinetic Modeling of Schema De-energizing vs. Network Building in Psychotherapy
ideas.description: This research applies computational pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling from pharmacology to formally test the hypothesis that de-energizing maladaptive schemas is less effective than building new adaptive networks. It models the 'dose' of a therapeutic intervention (e.g., cognitive restructuring vs. behavioral activation), its temporal 'concentration' in the cognitive-emotional system, and the resulting time-course of symptom change for each mechanism. This allows for the comparison of key parameters like the rate of therapeutic effect onset, the magnitude of peak effect, and the durability (half-life) of change, providing a dynamic, mechanistic test of the core theoretical assumption.

ideas.title: Dynamic Schema Mapping via Ecological Momentary Assessment for Transdiagnostic Model Validation
ideas.description: This research direction integrates Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) to test and refine an updated cognitive model across a range of psychological disorders (e.g., depression, GAD, PTSD). By using real-time, in-the-moment sampling, the study will capture the precise triggers, fluctuations, and consequences of specific schema activations and mode shifts as they unfold in daily life. This allows for a fine-grained, ecologically valid validation of the model's core mechanisms across different diagnostic presentations. The resulting data will not only test the model's transdiagnostic applicability but also dynamically inform its iterative development, revealing disorder-specific and common pathways in real time.

ideas.title: Apply computational modeling to treatment mechanisms
ideas.description: Use reinforcement learning models to formalize how cognitive and exposure therapies alter threat appraisal.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus exposure and applied relaxation in social phobia: A randomized controlled trial.

ideas.title: Scale down: Ultra-brief cognitive interventions
ideas.description: Develop and test a single-session or digital micro-intervention based on the core principles of the CT program.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus exposure and applied relaxation in social phobia: A randomized controlled trial.

ideas.title: Multi-Method Investigation of Implicit and Explicit Precursors to Maladaptive Schema Activation
ideas.description: This research program aims to dissect the precise triggers of maladaptive schema activation by moving beyond retrospective self-report. It utilizes a longitudinal design combining ecological momentary assessment, psychophysiological monitoring, and implicit behavioral tasks to capture schema reactivity in real-time and in response to subtle environmental cues. The core innovation is the systematic comparison of consciously accessible 'energizing' events with non-conscious physiological and behavioral precursors, testing for potential dissociations. This approach seeks to establish a more objective, mechanistic model of schema activation that can inform targeted intervention strategies.

ideas.title: Long-term follow-up beyond 15 months
ideas.description: Track the durability of cognitive therapy and applied relaxation effects for several years after treatment cessation.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Comparison of Cognitive Therapy, Applied Relaxation and Imipramine in the Treatment of Panic Disorder

ideas.title: Use ecological momentary assessment (EMA)
ideas.description: Apply EMA to sample appraisals and emotions in real-world social contexts for children with HFASD.
ideas.sourceTitle: Social phobia and interpretation of social events.

ideas.title: Use computational modeling from cognitive science
ideas.description: Apply computational models of learning and memory to predict individual response to exposure therapy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy for post-traumatic stress disorder: development and evaluation.

ideas.title: Applied relaxation is optimal arousal management
ideas.description: Challenge if applied relaxation is the best adjunct to exposure; test alternatives like mindfulness or acceptance.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus exposure and applied relaxation in social phobia: A randomized controlled trial.

ideas.title: A Homeostatic Model of Belief Regulation: Applying Immunological Tolerance to Cognitive Systems
ideas.description: This research investigates adaptive belief maintenance by drawing an analogy from immunology's solution to self/non-self discrimination. The immune system avoids harmful self-reactivity through a dual-layer tolerance system: central (developmental deletion of self-reactive cells) and peripheral (active suppression of escaped self-reactivity). We adapt this to model human belief systems. The study will formalize 'central belief tolerance' as the foundational cognitive schemas and learning rules that prevent the initial adoption of beliefs leading to extreme anxiety or dysfunction. It will then model 'peripheral belief tolerance' as the suite of dynamic, context-aware processes—akin to regulatory T cells and anergy—that modulate belief strength, compartmentalize contradictions, and facilitate adaptive updates without triggering systemic 'cognitive inflammation' (chronic anxiety or rigid dogmatism). The goal is to computationally specify the principles that allow a belief system to remain both robust to noise and flexibly adaptive to new evidence, thereby sustaining mental health.

ideas.title: Investigate neural correlates of appraisal processes
ideas.description: Use neuroimaging to link brain activity to specific cognitive appraisals in HFASD during social evaluation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Social phobia and interpretation of social events.

ideas.title: Long-term efficacy and relapse prevention comparisons
ideas.description: Compare the long-term durability and relapse rates of effective pharmacological and psychological interventions beyond acute treatment.
ideas.sourceTitle: Psychological and pharmacological interventions for social anxiety disorder in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis

ideas.title: Stress-Testing Cognitive Architectures: An External Framework for Evaluating Adaptive Belief Maintenance
ideas.description: This research establishes an independent, operationalized framework for adaptive belief maintenance, derived from clinical psychology and behavioral science, to serve as an external benchmark. It then subjects the target model's principles—alongside alternative cognitive architectures—to rigorous adversarial testing against this benchmark. The testing regimen includes simulated high-uncertainty environments, targeted belief perturbations, and longitudinal stress scenarios to measure outcomes like calibration drift, anxiety proxies, and flexibility. The goal is to falsifiably identify which principles, under which conditions, contribute to or detract from externally defined metrics of adaptive function.

ideas.title: Invert the direction of caregiver-child health link
ideas.description: Study how a child's TBI subsequently impacts the physical and mental health of the caregiver.
ideas.sourceTitle: A prospective investigation of the role of cognitive factors in persistent posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) after physical or sexual assault.

ideas.title: Use dose-response modeling from pharmacology
ideas.description: Apply dose-response and exposure-response modeling to psychological intervention components and intensity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Psychological and pharmacological interventions for social anxiety disorder in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis

ideas.title: Study long-term behavioral sequalae after mild TBI
ideas.description: Prospectively track behavioral and social outcomes following childhood mild TBI into adulthood.
ideas.sourceTitle: A prospective investigation of the role of cognitive factors in persistent posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) after physical or sexual assault.

ideas.title: Apply attentional blink paradigms
ideas.description: Use cognitive psychology attentional blink tasks to study the model's attentional focus component.
ideas.sourceTitle: Social Phobia: The Role of In-Situation Safety Behaviors in Maintaining Anxiety and Negative Beliefs

ideas.title: Mechanisms of CT's superior long-term effect
ideas.description: Identify the specific cognitive or behavioral mediators responsible for CT's sustained benefit over medication.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus fluoxetine in generalized social phobia: a randomized placebo-controlled trial.

ideas.title: Adapt gamification for self-exposure adherence
ideas.description: Use game design principles to increase engagement and compliance with self-exposure homework.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus fluoxetine in generalized social phobia: a randomized placebo-controlled trial.

ideas.title: Clarify causal pathways among processing, memory, and appraisals
ideas.description: Use advanced longitudinal designs to disentangle the temporal and causal relationships between the model's core constructs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Posttraumatic stress disorder following assault: the role of cognitive processing, trauma memory, and appraisals.

ideas.title: Micro-Tracking of Anxiety Dynamics via Ecological Momentary Assessment and Computational Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from computational neuroscience, specifically the modeling of neural spike trains as point processes and the inference of latent states from high-frequency data. The analogous problem—understanding how moment-to-moment neural activity gives rise to perception and behavior—is solved by treating neural events as discrete samples of a continuous, dynamic latent state. Adapted to clinical psychology, we propose using Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) to frequently sample 'micro-beliefs' and safety behaviors, treating each report as an event in a continuous-time stochastic process. By applying state-space and point-process models, we can infer the latent trajectory of state anxiety, identify immediate precursors (micro-beliefs) to safety behaviors, and model their dynamic interaction at a granular, moment-by-moment scale, moving beyond aggregate summaries to a true computational psychiatry approach.

ideas.title: Optimizing Applied Relaxation via Motor Skill Consolidation Principles
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from motor learning in sports science, where long-term skill retention is optimized through variable practice and spaced retrieval. The analogous solution involves training under changing conditions and recalling the skill after increasing delays to build robust, accessible neural pathways. Adapted to the research context, this means modifying applied relaxation protocols for panic disorder by incorporating varied practice environments (e.g., different times, postures, with minor distractions) and structured, spaced retrieval cues (e.g., a specific, non-panic trigger to initiate relaxation at unpredictable intervals). This approach aims to transform relaxation from a rehearsed technique into a consolidated 'motor skill' that is more automatically deployed during panic, potentially yielding greater long-term durability than cognitive therapy's reliance on verbalizable thought restructuring.

ideas.title: Long-term dynamics of cognitive-affective mode organization following therapeutic intervention
ideas.description: This study proposes a multi-year longitudinal follow-up of patients who have completed cognitive therapy or applied relaxation, tracking not just symptom relapse but also the hypothesized structural organization of their cognitive-affective modes (e.g., around expectancies, self-evaluations, or alternative core constructs). By assessing mode structure at multiple time points over several years post-treatment, the research can determine if sustained recovery is associated with a stable, reorganized mode architecture, or if symptom return coincides with a reversion to a pre-treatment organizational pattern. This directly tests whether therapeutic durability is a function of lasting structural change within the mode system, offering a novel mechanistic explanation for long-term outcomes.

ideas.title: Empirically test dual information processing additions
ideas.description: Design experiments to validate the role of dual information processing in the updated cognitive model.
ideas.sourceTitle: Social Phobia: The Role of In-Situation Safety Behaviors in Maintaining Anxiety and Negative Beliefs

ideas.title: A Computational Phenotyping Framework to Empirically Test Latent Non-Belief Constructs for Differential Prediction and Treatment Selection
ideas.description: This research program aims to move beyond the conceptual debate by building an empirical, data-driven framework to identify which specific non-belief factors are most predictive for disorder differentiation and treatment personalization. It employs a transdiagnostic longitudinal design, collecting dense behavioral, cognitive, and physiological data to derive latent computational parameters (e.g., reinforcement learning biases, attentional control metrics). The core test is whether these parameters significantly improve the prediction of differential diagnosis stability and treatment outcomes compared to models based solely on self-reported beliefs and symptoms. The ultimate goal is to generate a clinically actionable taxonomy based on mechanistic profiles rather than descriptive syndromes.

ideas.title: Disentangling Temporal from Mechanistic Drivers in Comparative Treatment Efficacy
ideas.description: This study investigates whether equalizing session duration eliminates outcome differences between cognitive therapy and imipramine, while directly testing the stronger rival hypothesis that other modality-specific therapeutic processes are the true drivers. The design employs three experimental arms (standard, time-equated, and dose-equated contact) to separate the confound of time from the active ingredients of each treatment. Intensive process tracking of mechanistic variables allows for causal mediation analysis to determine what factors, including but not limited to time, account for any observed outcome variance. This approach moves beyond a simple null hypothesis test to a explanatory model of how these treatments work, making the findings more robust and clinically informative.

ideas.title: Challenge pharmacological and psychological as distinct categories
ideas.description: Investigate mechanisms where pharmacological agents enhance psychological learning or vice versa.
ideas.sourceTitle: Psychological and pharmacological interventions for social anxiety disorder in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis

ideas.title: Therapist effects are negligible in manualized therapy
ideas.description: Investigate if specific therapist factors become significant predictors in larger, more diverse trials.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus exposure and applied relaxation in social phobia: A randomized controlled trial.

ideas.title: Computational Modeling of Disorder-Specific Maladaptive Schema Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction integrates a novel cognitive model with computational methods from network theory and dynamical systems to simulate the core maladaptive schema dynamics underlying specific psychological disorders (e.g., depression, PTSD, personality disorders). By formalizing disorder-specific schema networks—where nodes represent schemas and edges their associative strengths—the model can simulate how internal or external triggers 'energize' specific schema configurations, leading to predicted symptom states. This allows for hypothesis testing about disorder maintenance mechanisms and generates personalized, time-sensitive predictions for therapeutic interventions aimed at schema de-coupling and network reorganization.

ideas.title: Inverse Inference of Core Beliefs via Behavioral Spectroscopy
ideas.description: This research adapts the inverse problem-solving approach of astrophysical spectroscopy to the domain of cognitive-behavioral psychology. In astrophysics, the spectrum of light from a star is used to infer its hidden physical properties. Analogously, we propose treating an individual's observed behavioral and physiological responses across a calibrated set of contexts and threat cues as a 'behavioral spectrum.' By developing novel computational models (e.g., based on hierarchical Bayesian inference or spectral graph theory), the method would deconvolute this multi-dimensional behavioral pattern to reconstruct the latent architecture of core beliefs (e.g., schemas about self, world, and others) and their relative 'strengths' or 'abundances.' This provides a scalable, data-driven framework for moving from surface-level safety behaviors to the deep cognitive structures driving anxiety pathologies.

ideas.title: Ecological Momentary Psychophysiology for Dynamic Dual-Process Assessment
ideas.description: This research program integrates portable EEG or eye-tracking with smartphone-based ecological momentary assessment (EMA) to study dual-process models in naturalistic settings. Participants' automatic (e.g., attentional bias) and reflective (e.g., cognitive reappraisal) processes are indexed via psychophysiology in real-time, while simultaneous EMA prompts capture subjective experience and situational context. This allows for the identification of person-specific environmental or internal triggers that precipitate shifts from schema-driven automatic processing to more effortful reflective control. The resulting high-density, multi-modal dataset enables the testing of dynamic models of how cognitive modes fluctuate in daily life and their consequences for behavior and well-being.

ideas.title: A Dynamic Systems Approach to Multimodal Predictors of Long-Term Outcome
ideas.description: This research tests a integrative model where cognitive, physiological, and behavioral measures form a dynamic, interacting system that jointly predicts long-term outcome. Using intensive longitudinal data collection, it maps the temporal and causal relationships between these variable types with network analysis and advanced causal inference methods. The goal is to identify the most influential pathways or feedback loops within this system, rather than a single 'primary' mediator, that are most predictive of and causally responsible for long-term outcomes. This approach overcomes the reductionist flaw of pitting modalities against each other.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Construction to Develop Interventions for Cognitive Model Components
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from Ecology and Evolutionary Biology, specifically the concept of 'niche construction,' where organisms modify their environment and alter selection pressures. The analogous solution involves designing targeted environmental interventions (like beaver dam analogs) to shift an ecosystem's state by modifying its foundational components. Adapted to psychological research, this inspires the development of 'cognitive niche construction' as a therapeutic framework. The research would create and evaluate techniques designed to modify a patient's internal (e.g., thought patterns) and external (e.g., situational cues) environment with the precise goal of reshaping key model components like modes, attentional focus, and dual processing dynamics. The evaluation would assess how these targeted interventions alter the trajectory and stability of the overall cognitive-affective system.

ideas.title: Uniform support needs across all incident types
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that all critical incidents require the same type of psychological support.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive factors involved in the onset and maintenance of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) after physical or sexual assault.

ideas.title: Relax the requirement for routine outcome monitoring
ideas.description: Study whether mandatory session-by-session outcome measurement is necessary for clinical effectiveness or service improvement.
ideas.sourceTitle: Implementing NICE guidelines for the psychological treatment of depression and anxiety disorders: The IAPT experience

ideas.title: Active Causal Disambiguation of Latent Belief Models from Behavioral Trajectories
ideas.description: This research develops a formal framework to address the underdetermination inherent in inferring beliefs from behavior. It first uses passive observational data to generate a constrained equivalence class of minimal latent belief models that could explain an agent's safety behaviors and anxiety responses. The core innovation is an active learning component that designs and deploys efficient, ethical behavioral probes—subtle variations in context or task demands—to elicit discriminatory behavioral signatures. By observing responses to these probes, the system iteratively refines and prunes the candidate belief models, moving from mere description to causal explanation of the cognitive structures guiding behavior.

ideas.title: Predicting Long-Term Outcomes: A 'Biomarker vs. Symptom' Framework from Oncology
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that cognitive measures are the primary mediators of long-term outcome by drawing an analogy from oncology. In that field, the solution to improving prognostic accuracy involved moving from subjective symptom reports to objective biological markers. Adapting this solution, the research would systematically compare the predictive power of direct physiological/behavioral 'biomarkers' (e.g., autonomic nervous system activity, motor activity patterns, speech prosody) against traditional cognitive test scores for forecasting long-term real-world outcomes in conditions like schizophrenia, depression, or traumatic brain injury. The study design would involve longitudinal assessment, using machine learning or similar multivariate techniques to determine which class of measures—the 'biomarkers' or the 'cognitive symptoms'—carries more unique prognostic variance, thereby identifying the more fundamental mediator.

ideas.title: Direct head-to-head trials of top interventions
ideas.description: Conduct direct randomized trials comparing the most effective individual therapies, like individual CBT and SSRIs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Psychological and pharmacological interventions for social anxiety disorder in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis

ideas.title: Invert the role of peritraumatic dissociation
ideas.description: Explore if peritraumatic dissociation serves a protective function for some individuals in the acute aftermath.

ideas.title: Question individual therapy as the default format

ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy for post-traumatic stress disorder: development and evaluation.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: What maintains treatment gains?
ideas.description: Study the behaviors and cognitions that protect against relapse after successful CT.

ideas.title: Test the proposed prevention and intervention protocol
ideas.description: Empirically evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed support protocol for train engineers after critical incidents.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive factors involved in the onset and maintenance of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) after physical or sexual assault.

ideas.title: Attentional Robustness Stress-Testing via Controlled Intervention on RSVP Processing
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond the flawed human analogy to directly probe the transformer's attentional mechanics. It employs a causal intervention framework where attention weights are systematically perturbed during the model's processing of Rapid Serial Visual Presentation (RSVP) streams. The core measurement is the performance degradation on target identification tasks as a function of the timing and magnitude of these interventions, creating an 'attentional vulnerability profile'. This profile is then analyzed against the model's architectural features to identify which components are most critical for robust, sequential information processing under conflict.

ideas.title: Mapping the Transition from Adaptive to Maladaptive Modes via Network Dynamics
ideas.description: This research investigates whether the organization of cognitive-affective modes—conceptualized as dynamic, self-reinforcing states—can be mapped along a continuum from adaptive to maladaptive function across disorders. It challenges the assumption that modes are primarily organized around expectancies and self-evaluations, instead examining alternative organizational principles (e.g., attractor strength, network connectivity patterns, or interoceptive feedback loops) that may govern mode stability and switching. The core aim is to determine if the transition into psychopathology is marked by a quantifiable change in these underlying organizational dynamics, revealing how normal, flexible mode transitions degrade into rigid, maladaptive ones. This approach moves beyond descriptive content to identify the formal system properties that differentiate health from disorder.

ideas.title: Context-Aware, Minimally-Intrusive Digital Phenotyping for Panic State Detection and Intervention
ideas.description: This redesigned system combines passive, continuous data collection from smartphone sensors and wearables with a context-sensitive, adaptive EMA protocol. It first establishes a personal baseline and identifies individualized digital biomarkers correlated with self-reported panic during a calibration phase. The system then primarily operates in a passive monitoring mode, using these biomarkers to detect probable panic episodes. It initiates ultra-brief, symptom-specific EMA prompts only when detection confidence is high, and immediately offers a micro-intervention (e.g., grounding exercise) to counteract any assessment-induced distress. This approach minimizes participant burden and reactivity while maximizing ecological validity and clinical utility.

ideas.title: Adopt network meta-analysis from pharmacology
ideas.description: Use network meta-analysis to rank all PTSD therapies, including those never directly compared.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy for post-traumatic stress disorder: development and evaluation.

ideas.description: Explore if peer support or fully automated systems can achieve similar outcomes for certain populations.
ideas.sourceTitle: Implementing NICE guidelines for the psychological treatment of depression and anxiety disorders: The IAPT experience

ideas.title: Fidelity analysis from psychotherapy process research
ideas.description: Apply rigorous therapy adherence and competence rating scales to ensure treatment differentiation.
ideas.sourceTitle: A Comparison of Cognitive Therapy, Applied Relaxation and Imipramine in the Treatment of Panic Disorder

ideas.title: Longitudinal study of reaction development models
ideas.description: Conduct a long-term study to validate the proposed models of traumatic reaction development in railway personnel.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive factors involved in the onset and maintenance of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) after physical or sexual assault.

ideas.title: Scale up: public health prevention campaigns
ideas.description: Design population-level mental health literacy campaigns to reduce stigma and encourage help-seeking.
ideas.sourceTitle: Psychological and pharmacological interventions for social anxiety disorder in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis

ideas.title: Long-term durability tracking using ecological momentary assessment and booster session triggers
ideas.description: This research adapts the vaccine durability monitoring approach from vaccinology to psychotherapy outcome studies. Just as vaccine research tracks antibody titers over years with booster shots administered when protection wanes, this study would implement ecological momentary assessment (EMA) via smartphone to monitor symptom trajectories for 5+ years after cognitive therapy or applied relaxation treatment ends. The methodology would establish quantitative relapse thresholds that trigger automated alerts to participants and clinicians, offering optional 'booster' therapy sessions when symptoms approach predefined levels. This creates a novel hybrid model combining long-term durability tracking with a just-in-time intervention framework, allowing researchers to distinguish between natural durability decay and preventable relapse while maintaining ethical responsibility to participants.

ideas.title: Scale up to national or multi-railway study
ideas.description: Conduct a large-scale, multi-company study to generalize findings on post-incident reactions.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive factors involved in the onset and maintenance of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) after physical or sexual assault.

ideas.title: Replicate findings with larger, more diverse samples
ideas.description: Confirm the correlation and regression results with a bigger cohort of children with HFASD.
ideas.sourceTitle: Social phobia and interpretation of social events.

ideas.title: Computational Pharmacodynamic Modeling of Post-Imipramine Relapse Trajectories
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the search for relapse predictors with computational PK/PD modeling to develop a novel class of pharmacodynamic models for relapse. These models would treat the post-withdrawal period as a dynamic system, where patient characteristics (e.g., genetic markers, baseline symptom severity, treatment duration) act as parameters influencing the modeled 'washout' of the drug's protective effect and the subsequent 'rebound' of depressive symptoms. This allows for the prediction of not just who is at risk, but the likely timing and severity of relapse for an individual patient. The approach provides a quantitative, mechanistic bridge between static clinical predictors and the dynamic biological and psychological processes underlying relapse.

ideas.title: Adaptive Neurocognitive Optimization: Using Early Physiological/Behavioral Signals to Personalize Cognitive Interventions
ideas.description: This research synthesizes the search for superior early predictors of long-term outcome with the adaptive treatment strategy framework from oncology. It proposes a Sequential Multiple Assignment Randomized Trial (SMART) design for cognitive or mental health interventions where the decision to adapt treatment (e.g., intensify, switch, or maintain) is triggered not by traditional cognitive test scores, but by early changes in objective physiological (e.g., neural activity, autonomic) or passive behavioral (e.g., digital phenotyping) measures. This allows for more rapid and mechanistically informed personalization than waiting for cognitive changes, potentially leading to more efficacious, individually optimized intervention sequences. The core hypothesis is that these objective early mediators will enable more accurate dynamic treatment regimens, improving long-term functional outcomes.

ideas.title: Incident frequency does not affect baseline risk
ideas.description: Challenge the assumption that prior incident exposure does not alter future reaction severity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive factors involved in the onset and maintenance of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) after physical or sexual assault.

ideas.title: Cost-effectiveness analysis of CT versus fluoxetine
ideas.description: Compare the long-term economic and healthcare utilization costs of cognitive therapy versus pharmacotherapy.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus fluoxetine in generalized social phobia: a randomized placebo-controlled trial.

ideas.title: Quantifying Phase Transitions in Psychopathology Using Dynamical Systems Theory
ideas.description: This research seed explores the continuity between adaptive and maladaptive function by drawing an analogy from materials science and physics. The analogous problem is understanding phase transitions, where a system shifts from one stable state to another (e.g., liquid to solid) via continuous changes in a controlling variable. This is solved using dynamical systems theory, identifying order parameters that characterize each phase and modeling the bifurcation points. Adapted to psychopathology, the idea is to formally model mental disorders as phase transitions in a complex system. The research would define measurable order parameters (like specific patterns of amygdala-prefrontal connectivity or rigidity in behavioral repertoires) and control parameters (like chronic stress or inflammatory markers). By applying bifurcation analysis and catastrophe theory, the goal is to mathematically describe the tipping points where normal adaptive processes (such as acute fear or sadness) lose stability and transition into maladaptive, self-perpetuating pathological states seen across disorders, providing a unified framework for prevention and intervention.

ideas.title: Personal Dynamical Systems Modeling for Adaptive Psychotherapy Scheduling and Intervention Selection
ideas.description: This approach constructs patient-specific computational models of psychological functioning from high-frequency longitudinal data, capturing each individual's unique regulatory dynamics and vulnerabilities. Instead of imposing a pharmacological analogy, it uses these identified dynamical systems to simulate how different therapeutic interventions, modeled as targeted perturbations to the system's control parameters, would alter future state trajectories. The system then operates as a clinical decision-support tool, running in-session simulations to recommend the optimal timing and type of therapeutic action (e.g., when to introduce a challenging cognitive restructuring task versus a supportive skill) for that individual at that moment. This enables truly adaptive, personalized psychotherapy that is responsive to the patient's evolving internal state and predicted response pathways.

ideas.title: All patients benefit equally from the same treatment
ideas.description: Identify patient characteristics that predict better response to CT versus medication.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive therapy versus fluoxetine in generalized social phobia: a randomized placebo-controlled trial.

ideas.title: Scale down to micro-interactions in post-incident care
ideas.description: Analyze the micro-dynamics of support conversations between managers and affected employees.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive factors involved in the onset and maintenance of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) after physical or sexual assault.

ideas.title: Symmetrical Schema Energy Modulation: De-energizing vs. Energizing vs. Balanced Protocols
ideas.description: This research tests the 'schema energizing assumption' by directly comparing the therapeutic efficacy of symmetrical, targeted interventions on neural schema networks. Using fMRI-neurofeedback or repetitive TMS, we will create three conditions: selectively reducing activation in a maladaptive schema network, selectively increasing activation in a pre-trained adaptive schema, and a dual-pathway condition that does both concurrently. The primary outcome is the resilience of the adaptive response under stress, measured behaviorally and neurally, allowing a clean test of whether change is best driven by subtraction, addition, or their integration.

ideas.title: Multi-Modal Passive Sensing with Model-Guided Sparse Sampling for Mode & Schema Detection
ideas.description: This system employs a suite of passive smartphone and wearable sensors to continuously and unobtrusively capture behavioral, contextual, and physiological data streams indicative of cognitive-affective states. A computational model analyzes these fused signals to detect probable patterns of mode shifts and schema-activating contexts, moving beyond pure self-report. The system then uses these model inferences to trigger brief, context-sensitive ecological momentary assessments only at moments of high predictive certainty, thereby validating the model and collecting targeted self-report while minimizing participant burden and reactivity. This approach provides a more objective, continuous, and less intrusive measurement framework for studying dynamic psychological processes.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: from treatment response to mechanism
ideas.description: Use differential treatment response patterns to reverse-engineer and validate underlying neurocognitive mechanisms.
ideas.sourceTitle: Psychological and pharmacological interventions for social anxiety disorder in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis

ideas.title: Mechanism-First Intervention Development via Computational Model Falsification
ideas.description: This research program first formalizes a target therapeutic mechanism (e.g., shifting from a 'threat' mode to a 'compassionate' mode) as a testable computational model, such as a change in hierarchical policy priors within a reinforcement learning framework. Novel interventions are then algorithmically derived from this model to specifically manipulate its parameters. The efficacy of these techniques is evaluated in controlled experiments that measure both clinical outcomes and computational process variables, with the explicit goal of falsifying the mechanistic model to either validate it or force a more precise theoretical revision.

ideas.title: A Predictive Test of Interpersonal Motivational Systems as an Alternative Organizing Principle for Modes
ideas.description: This research program proposes and rigorously tests a specific alternative to the expectancy/self-evaluation organization of modes, positing that modes are primarily organized around core interpersonal motivational systems (e.g., care-seeking, rank, affiliation). It will construct a computational network model of modes based on this principle using intensive longitudinal data. The predictive and explanatory validity of this model will be directly compared to the incumbent theory by forecasting real-world behavioral outcomes and testing causal predictions through targeted micro-interventions, providing a definitive, falsifiable test of a concrete alternative organization.

ideas.title: Scale up: Population-level screening using brief cognitive markers
ideas.description: Develop ultra-brief measures of peritraumatic processing for large-scale screening in emergency departments to triage risk.
ideas.sourceTitle: Posttraumatic stress disorder following assault: the role of cognitive processing, trauma memory, and appraisals.

ideas.title: Apply human factors analysis to incident reporting
ideas.description: Use human factors and systems analysis frameworks to understand incident precursors and responses.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive factors involved in the onset and maintenance of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) after physical or sexual assault.

ideas.title: Empirical dose-response meta-analysis to define the optimal imipramine comparator range
ideas.description: This study conducts a systematic review and dose-response meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials of imipramine for major depressive disorder from the 1960s to 2005. Using pre-specified criteria for optimal dose (peak efficacy with acceptable discontinuation rates, aligned with historical expert consensus guidelines), it models the dose-response curve to identify the evidence-based therapeutic range. The mean dose used in STAR*D (233 mg/day) is then statistically compared to this empirically derived optimal range, providing a quantitative, rather than conceptual, challenge to its status as the ideal pharmacological comparator.

ideas.description: Develop and test single-session or very brief digital interventions as a pre-IAPT or community-wide public health offering.
ideas.sourceTitle: Implementing NICE guidelines for the psychological treatment of depression and anxiety disorders: The IAPT experience

ideas.title: Active-Cohort Durability Study with Multi-Modal Follow-Up and Planned Recapture Assessments
ideas.description: This study proactively tracks the long-term trajectory of cognitive therapy and applied relaxation effects by implementing an active, multi-source measurement system designed to combat attrition. Instead of relying solely on intermittent self-report, it employs a low-burden core of annual digital check-ins and, where consented, passive behavioral data collection, while linking to administrative health records for objective healthcare utilization outcomes. The design's core innovation is the pre-scheduled, well-resourced 'recapture' assessments at major intervals (e.g., years 3, 5, 7), which aim to re-engage even temporarily lost participants, ensuring the final sample remains representative of the original randomized cohort. This approach yields a robust, longitudinal dataset that distinguishes true treatment durability from artifacts of selective participant retention.

ideas.title: Cognitive Pruning vs. Strengthening: A Neuroplasticity Analogy
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from developmental neurobiology, where synaptic pruning is essential for creating efficient neural circuits, this idea inverts the schema energizing assumption. The analogous solution is the brain's dual-process of eliminating inefficient connections (pruning) while strengthening useful ones. Adapted to cognitive therapy research, the proposed study tests if 'cognitive pruning'—facilitating the atrophy of maladaptive schemas through disuse and redirected engagement—is more effective than interventions aimed at directly de-energizing them. The core hypothesis is that building robust new adaptive networks naturally outcompetes and prunes the old schema pathways, offering a less confrontational and more neurologically plausible route to lasting change.

ideas.title: Apply control theory from engineering to model and optimize psychological treatment dosing
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Control Systems Engineering, where feedback loops and system models (e.g., PID controllers) are used to dynamically adjust inputs to maintain a desired output state. The analogous problem—optimizing a dynamic system's performance—is solved by continuously measuring the output, comparing it to a target, and calculating corrective adjustments to the input. Adapted to psychological treatment, the patient's symptom severity is the system output, and therapeutic interventions (session frequency, modality, homework) are the control inputs. A computational model, informed by initial patient characteristics and ongoing symptom feedback, would function as an adaptive controller. It would provide data-driven recommendations for adjusting the 'dose' of treatment components over time, aiming to optimize the speed, magnitude, and stability of therapeutic improvement, similar to how an engineering system is tuned for optimal performance.

ideas.title: Controlling for 'Dose' in Comparative Trials: A Pharmacology-Inspired Protocol
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from pharmacology, where comparing drug efficacy requires standardizing for total biological exposure (AUC). The analogous solution is to match the 'dose' of different interventions based on their systemic impact over time. Adapted to psychotherapy research, this means designing a trial to investigate if equalizing the total 'therapeutic contact time' (session duration x frequency) per week between cognitive therapy and pharmacotherapy (imipramine) eliminates their outcome differences. The imipramine arm would include supportive clinical management sessions of equal length and frequency to the therapy sessions, and the therapy sessions would be duration-capped, thereby testing if the 'dose' of clinician time, rather than the intervention type, is the primary driver of differential outcomes.

ideas.title: Apply psychophysiological measures to dual processing
ideas.description: Use EEG or eye-tracking to index automatic vs. reflective processing posited by the model.
ideas.sourceTitle: Social Phobia: The Role of In-Situation Safety Behaviors in Maintaining Anxiety and Negative Beliefs

ideas.title: A Convergent Psychophysiological and Behavioral Modeling Framework to Disentangle Dual-Process Engagement
ideas.description: This research employs a multi-method triangulation strategy to move beyond simple reverse inference in testing dual-process theories. It integrates temporally sensitive EEG, pupil dilation and microsaccade metrics from eye-tracking, and a carefully designed behavioral interference paradigm within a single experimental session. The core innovation is the use of formal computational modeling to evaluate how the joint pattern of all measured variables shifts under conditions designed to selectively burden automatic versus reflective processing, thereby providing a robust, system-level signature of engagement rather than relying on any single biomarker.

ideas.description: Employ twin registries to disentangle genetic and environmental contributions to TBI risk and outcomes.
ideas.sourceTitle: A prospective investigation of the role of cognitive factors in persistent posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) after physical or sexual assault.

ideas.title: Adapting fMRI Decoding Techniques to Isolate Dual Cognitive Processes
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from cognitive neuroscience, where fMRI-based multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) decodes distinct neural representations of co-active cognitive processes. The analogous solution involves designing tasks to isolate processes, training classifiers on neural patterns from pure conditions, and testing them on mixed conditions to validate dissociable representations. Adapted to the context of validating a dual information processing cognitive model, the research would design experiments to obtain behavioral and psychophysiological markers (e.g., response time distributions, eye-gaze patterns, pupil dilation) from tasks that are prototypical for each hypothesized processing stream. Machine learning classifiers would be trained on this data to create signatures for each stream. The core empirical test would involve administering novel, complex tasks where the model predicts integrated or competing contributions from both streams, and using the classifiers to detect the presence and timing of each stream's engagement, thereby providing empirical validation for the dual-process architecture.

ideas.title: Adversarial Strong Inference Testing of Competing Cognitive Architectures
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond confirmatory testing of a single model by implementing a strong inference framework. It defines a set of core, measurable phenomena related to information processing that any cognitive architecture must explain. The system then designs pre-registered, critical experiments where the dual-process model and at least two viable alternative theoretical models make distinct, falsifiable predictions. Through adversarial collaboration and convergent evidence from behavioral tasks, neuroimaging (fMRI/EEG), and computational modeling, the research aims to competitively evaluate which architectural framework best accounts for the empirical data, rigorously testing the necessity of the proposed dual-process additions.

ideas.title: Reactions follow a single developmental model
ideas.description: Challenge that a single model explains all post-incident reaction trajectories.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive factors involved in the onset and maintenance of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) after physical or sexual assault.

ideas.title: Dual: Study impact on families of employees
ideas.description: Examine the secondary traumatic stress experienced by families of engineers after critical incidents.
ideas.sourceTitle: Cognitive factors involved in the onset and maintenance of posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) after physical or sexual assault.

ideas.title: Adaptive Dosing in Psychotherapy: A Pharmacokinetic Model for Session Frequency and Duration
ideas.description: This idea challenges the fixed 12-session therapy protocol by drawing an analogy from clinical pharmacology. In that field, the solution to rigid dosing regimens was therapeutic drug monitoring and pharmacokinetic modeling, which personalizes treatment based on individual metabolism and response. Adapted to psychotherapy, the research would develop a model where the 'therapeutic dose' is a dynamic variable. By measuring early-treatment 'response kinetics' (symptom change, therapeutic alliance, skill acquisition), the model would algorithmically recommend an adaptive treatment plan—varying total sessions, their spacing, and possibly content intensity—to achieve a predefined therapeutic endpoint. The core test is whether this pharmacokinetically-inspired, adaptive dosing protocol leads to better, more efficient outcomes than the standard 12-session fixed course.

ideas.title: Dual Processing as a Dynamic Continuum: A Phase Transition Model
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that dual-process theories in psychology describe two separate, distinct cognitive systems. Drawing an analogy from condensed matter physics, where the distinct states of matter (solid, liquid, gas) are understood as phases of a single system connected by continuous transitions, we adapt this solution to cognitive science. The analogous solution involved moving from a categorical model to a continuum model defined by order parameters and external conditions. The adapted idea proposes modeling cognitive processing not as a dichotomy between System 1 and System 2, but as a single, unified processing system whose operational mode exists on a dynamic continuum. The specific 'phase' or style of processing (from highly automatic to deeply controlled) is determined by a set of interacting cognitive parameters—such as attentional resources, time pressure, expertise, and motivational state—which can induce smooth or abrupt shifts along the continuum, analogous to phase transitions. This framework allows for hybrid processing states and predicts the conditions under which transitions occur, offering a more nuanced and testable model of human reasoning and decision-making.

ideas.title: Apply computational psychiatry models to treatment prediction

ideas.title: Quantifying the Cognitive Continuum: A Test for a Unitary Latent Parameter Governing Processing Mode
ideas.description: This research program tests the strong hypothesis that a single, continuous latent parameter (e.g., neural gain or competition threshold) governs the balance between heuristic and analytic processing, creating the appearance of discrete systems. We develop a computational model that generates distinct behavioral and neural signatures for a continuum versus discrete systems. These signatures are then tested using EEG/MEG to track the temporal dynamics of this parameter during task switching, and pharmacological interventions (e.g., low-dose GABA agonists) are used to experimentally shift the parameter along its continuum, providing causal evidence for its unitary nature.

ideas.title: Challenge waitlist as the only control
ideas.description: Evaluate interventions against active placebos or supportive therapy instead of passive waitlist controls.
ideas.sourceTitle: Psychological and pharmacological interventions for social anxiety disorder in adults: a systematic review and network meta-analysis

ideas.title: Apply Radar Signal Processing CFAR Techniques to Model Attentional Blink Analysis
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from radar signal processing. The analogous problem is distinguishing true targets from noise in a cluttered environment, which is solved using Constant False Alarm Rate (CFAR) algorithms that dynamically adjust detection thresholds. The adapted research idea applies this principle to the analysis of a model's performance on attentional blink tasks. By implementing a CFAR-inspired adaptive threshold on the model's internal attention metrics or output error rates, researchers can more accurately and reliably identify periods of 'attentional blink' by separating genuine lapses in attentional focus from the inherent noise and variability in the model's processing, leading to a more precise characterization of its attentional bottleneck dynamics.

ideas.title: Adaptive Ecological Momentary Assessment with Triggered Sampling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to monitoring transient astronomical events for the problem of tracking psychological mode shifts and schema activation. In astronomy, systems like the Swift satellite use a two-stage, triggered approach: a wide-field detector identifies a potential event, which automatically triggers targeted, high-resolution follow-up observations. Adapted to behavioral medicine, this becomes an adaptive Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) system. A base layer of passive, continuous sensing from wearable devices or smartphones (analogous to the wide-field instrument) monitors for behavioral or physiological signatures indicative of a potential psychological shift. When a trigger threshold is crossed, the system automatically deploys a customized, in-depth EMA protocol to the participant's device. This method increases ecological validity and data richness by capturing self-reports proximal to the event, while improving efficiency and reducing participant burden by replacing many random prompts with targeted, event-contingent sampling.

ideas.title: Reorganizing Psychological Modes via a Network Topology Model
ideas.description: This research challenges the mode theory's assumption that modes are fundamentally organized around expectancies and self-evaluations. Drawing an analogy from ecology and systems biology, where the organization of species in an ecosystem was re-conceptualized from simple trophic hierarchies to complex interaction networks, we propose a similar shift. The analogous solution used network topology analysis to uncover organization based on connectivity, modularity, and stability patterns. Adapted to psychology, this involves investigating modes as elements in a dynamic network, mapping their connections via temporal sequencing, co-occurrence, and functional relationships. This approach may reveal that modes are organized by principles of system stability, transition probabilities, and functional modules (e.g., threat-response clusters), offering an alternative, potentially more predictive, structural model than one centered on self-referential evaluations.

ideas.title: Quantifying Phase Transitions from Adaptive to Maladaptive Function Using Computational Phenotyping
ideas.description: This research program first establishes operational, quantifiable definitions of adaptive and maladaptive function that are independent of diagnostic labels, based on evolutionary theory and normative data. It then employs intensive longitudinal multimodal phenotyping (digital behavior, physiology, neuroimaging) in transdiagnostic at-risk cohorts to collect dense temporal data on candidate processes. Finally, it uses dynamical systems and machine learning models to identify precise, predictive thresholds or bifurcation points where the cost-benefit ratio of an adaptive process inverts, rigorously testing these models for their ability to forecast future disorder onset and severity.

ideas.title: Predictive, Biologically Constrained Dynamical Modeling of Schema Activation
ideas.description: This research integrates computational modeling from network theory and dynamical systems with empirical cognitive neuroscience to create testable, predictive accounts of schema dynamics. The approach begins by identifying robust neural or behavioral signatures of schema states from task-based data. A dynamical model is then constructed, with its parameters and architecture constrained by these empirical priors, and is used to simulate the temporal evolution of schema activation. The model's critical output is a set of novel, quantifiable predictions about subsequent neural patterns or behavioral consequences, which are rigorously tested against new experimental data to validate or falsify the theoretical framework.

ideas.title: SMART Designs to Deconstruct and Optimize the Pharmacological Comparator in Depression Trials
ideas.description: This research applies Sequential Multiple Assignment Randomized Trial (SMART) designs from oncology to challenge the fixed-dose comparator paradigm in depression trials. Instead of accepting a single mean dose (e.g., 233 mg/day imipramine) as the optimal pharmacological control, the SMART framework is used to randomize non-responders at an early stage to different comparator dose strategies (e.g., dose continuation, dose escalation, or switch). This allows for the direct empirical comparison of which dosing strategy for the traditional comparator leads to the best ultimate outcomes, thereby personalizing the control arm itself and generating robust, practical evidence on what truly constitutes an optimal active comparator for future efficacy trials.

ideas.title: Predicting relapse after imipramine withdrawal using ecological resilience biomarkers and network stability analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from ecology, where the risk of ecosystem collapse after a disturbance is predicted using resilience biomarkers and network stability analysis. The analogous problem is predicting system failure after removing a key component. The adapted idea is to identify patient characteristics predictive of relapse after imipramine discontinuation by modeling the patient as a complex system. Pre-withdrawal multivariate biomarkers (e.g., neurophysiological, endocrine, cognitive, and social network measures) will be analyzed using dynamical systems models to quantify the stability and resilience of the patient's remission state, thereby predicting relapse risk upon medication withdrawal.

ideas.title: Apply High-Frequency Financial Market Microstructure Analysis to Index Dual-Process Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from financial market microstructure analysis, where high-frequency data is used to decompose market activity into fast algorithmic trading and slower fundamental trading. The analogous solution identifies temporal and correlational signatures in order flow to index these components. Adapted to cognitive neuroscience, the research would use high-density EEG to decompose neural activity into signatures of automatic versus reflective processing. Specifically, it would treat neural responses as a 'market' of cognitive operations, using the temporal microstructure (latency, adaptation, cross-regional coupling) of EEG features like event-related potentials and oscillatory power to develop quantifiable indices. Fast, invariant responses would index the automatic system, while slower, context-modulated, and frontally coordinated patterns would index the reflective system, allowing for a dynamic, trial-by-trial assay of dual-process engagement.

ideas.title: Adaptive Learning Pathways in Education
ideas.description: This idea applies the solution to an analogous problem from the field of Educational Technology, specifically Intelligent Tutoring Systems. The analogous solution involves continuously assessing student performance and dynamically adjusting subsequent learning material to branch students onto personalized instructional pathways. Adapted to the research context of adaptive treatment strategies, this translates to a Sequential Multiple Assignment Randomized Trial (SMART) design for mental health interventions. Patient response is measured frequently using digital tools like ecological momentary assessment (EMA), and based on this early feedback, the treatment algorithm randomizes patients to different subsequent therapy options (e.g., switching modality, adjusting intensity) to personalize and optimize the therapeutic pathway in a data-driven manner.

ideas.title: Challenging the 'Standard Dose' as an Optimal Comparator Using Agricultural Dose-Response Modeling
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the mean 233 mg/day imipramine dose was the optimal pharmacological comparator by drawing an analogy from agronomy. In crop science, the optimal dose of an input is determined through dose-response modeling, not by assuming the average applied dose is ideal. The analogous solution is to use experimental data to fit models that identify the dose producing a specific target effect. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to treat the 233 mg/day dose as an unvalidated point on a curve. By conducting a systematic review and meta-regression of historical clinical trial data, we can model imipramine's dose-response relationship for key outcomes. This analysis would empirically test whether 233 mg/day is the optimal therapeutic dose for comparison or if the comparator benchmark should be recalibrated to a dose identified by the model as having the best efficacy-tolerability profile.

ideas.title: Modeling Schema Activation as a Phase Transition in a Spin Glass System
ideas.description: This idea adapts computational models from statistical physics, specifically spin glass theory, to simulate schema dynamics in cognition. The analogous problem in physics involves modeling how a disordered magnetic system (a spin glass) settles into a stable configuration through a phase transition, solved using Hamiltonian-based energy landscapes and stochastic simulation methods like Monte Carlo. In the adapted research idea, a cognitive schema network is represented as a spin glass system, where concepts are spins and associative strengths are interaction couplings. The 'energizing' or activation of a specific schema is computationally modeled as the system transitioning to a coherent, low-energy state under the influence of an external contextual field. By simulating this process, we can explore the stability, switching, and competition between schemas as a function of network connectivity and external input, providing a formal, quantitative model of schema dynamics derived from physical principles.

ideas.title: Closed-loop, model-informed adaptive therapeutics using continuous multi-modal data assimilation
ideas.description: This system moves beyond fixed-interval, biomarker-triggered adaptations by establishing a closed-loop learning framework. It continuously assimilates high-frequency, multi-modal patient data (e.g., ctDNA, serial imaging, proteomics, digital biomarkers) into a mechanistic or deep probabilistic disease model. A reinforcement learning agent, trained on both this real-time patient state and a synthetic cohort generated by the model, recommends the optimal therapeutic intervention at any decision point. This enables truly personalized, dynamic treatment strategies that adapt to the evolving, multi-factorial landscape of an individual's disease and response, maximizing long-term outcomes.
